message.content:
Independent verifier for GitHub issue #20 in /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch main). You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#20, stated HIGH, "incomplete fix of #2"): In confirm.py, the unit-level path (verdict_from_pvalue / confirm_unit_level) decides supported-vs-contradicted entirely from a caller-supplied direction_supports_claim boolean the engine never cross-checks against the statistic. A statistic in the contradicting tail can be reported SUPPORTED. evidence doesn't record the direction flag. Repro: verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=-99.0, p_value=0.001, alpha=0.05, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True) → SUPPORTED.

Verify the repro and read confirm.py. Consider counter-arguments: for a unit-level held-out test, is direction legitimately the caller's to compute (the statistic's "sign" may not map to claim direction without a reference)? Is requiring the engine to derive it feasible, or would it impose an unwanted model? Is HIGH right, or lower since the caller is the trusted baseline toolkit? Decide.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high|medium|low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding. Let me start by reading confirm.py and running the repro.

content.content:
1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import math
27	from typing import Iterable, Literal
28	
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
31	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
32	
33	Direction = Literal["greater", "less"]
34	
35	
36	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
37	    """Gate: a hypothesis must be locked + timestamped before any confirmatory step."""
38	    if not hypothesis.locked:
39	        raise FirewallViolation(
40	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
41	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
42	        )
43	
44	
45	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
46	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
47	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
48	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
49	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
50	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
51	
52	
53	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
54	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
55	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
56	
57	
58	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
59	    if not math.isfinite(value):
60	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
61	
62	
63	def verdict_from_pvalue(
64	    hypothesis_id: str,
65	    *,
66	    statistic: float,
67	    p_value: float,
68	    alpha: float,
69	    powered: bool,
70	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
71	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
72	) -> Verdict:
73	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
74	
75	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
76	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
77	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
78	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
79	
80	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
81	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the
82	    recorded decision rule reflects the caller's ``test_description`` rather than a hard-coded
83	    (and previously incorrect) sidedness label (issue #2).
84	    """
85	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
86	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
87	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
88	
89	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
90	    common = dict(statistic=statistic, threshold=alpha, evidence={"p_value": p_value})
91	    if not powered:
92	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
93	    if p_value <= alpha:
94	        if direction_supports_claim:
95	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
96	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
97	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
98	        hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common
99	    )
100	
101	
102	def confirm_unit_level(
103	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
104	    *,
105	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
106	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
107	    statistic: float,
108	    p_value: float,
109	    alpha: float,
110	    powered: bool,
111	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
112	) -> Verdict:
113	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only."""
114	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
115	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
116	    require_locked(hypothesis)
117	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
118	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
119	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
120	        statistic=statistic,
121	        p_value=p_value,
122	        alpha=alpha,
123	        powered=powered,
124	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
125	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
126	    )
127	
128	
129	def _empirical_p(null: list[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
130	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
131	    n = len(null)
132	    if tail == "greater":
133	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
134	    else:
135	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
136	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
137	
138	
139	def confirm_whole_corpus(
140	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
141	    *,
142	    observed: float,
143	    null_distribution: list[float],
144	    alpha: float,
145	    stable_across_resamples: bool,
146	    predicted_direction: Direction,
147	) -> Verdict:
148	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + stability check.
149	
150	    ``predicted_direction`` is the tail the claim predicts the observed statistic lies in
151	    (``"greater"`` or ``"less"`` than the null). The pattern must (a) beat the null at
152	    ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and (b) be stable across resamples → ``supported``. A
153	    result significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``. Anything else (unstable,
154	    or not significant in either tail) → ``indeterminate``.
155	    """
156	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
157	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
158	    require_locked(hypothesis)
159	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
160	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
161	    if not null_distribution:
162	        raise ValueError("a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation")
163	    for v in null_distribution:
164	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
165	
166	    opposite: Direction = "less" if predicted_direction == "greater" else "greater"
167	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, predicted_direction)
168	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
169	    n = len(null_distribution)
170	    rule = (
171	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail "
172	        f"'{predicted_direction}'; requires stability across resamples"
173	    )
174	    evidence = {
175	        "p_support": p_support,
176	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
177	        "null_n": n,
178	        "stable": stable_across_resamples,
179	        "predicted_direction": predicted_direction,
180	    }
181	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
182	
183	    if not stable_across_resamples:
184	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
185	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
186	        )
187	    if p_support <= alpha:
188	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
189	    if p_contra <= alpha:
190	        return Verdict.contradicted(
191	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
192	            rule,
193	            notes="significant in the opposite tail",
194	            **common,
195	        )
196	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
197	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
198	    )
199

content.text: Repro confirmed. The statistic `-99.0` with `direction_supports_claim=True` yields SUPPORTED. The `direction_supports_claim` flag is not recorded in evidence. Let me examine the whole-corpus path for contrast and look at issue #2's original fix and the GitHub issues.

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me understand the design intent by comparing the two paths closely. The key analytical question is whether the unit-level path's design is intentional and defensible.

Let me check the METHODOLOGY.md reference and the docstring claims about how p_value is computed for the unit-level case.

content.content:
ARCHITECTURE.md
CHARTER.md
GLOSSARY.md
GOVERNANCE.md
METHODOLOGY.md
decisions
protocol
./docs/METHODOLOGY.md

13:descriptive statistics. It is:
45:For unit-level claims this is a **held-out split**. For whole-corpus claims (where you
70:- **Supported** — the baseline corroborates the claim/hypothesis at the pre-specified
72:- **Contradicted** — the baseline is inconsistent with the claim at the pre-specified

40	
41	- discover on an exploration partition (or via descriptive analysis), lock the now-specific
42	  claim, then
43	- confirm on data the discovery never touched.
44	
45	For unit-level claims this is a **held-out split**. For whole-corpus claims (where you
46	cannot hold out part of the object — e.g. global structure or coverage), confirmation is
47	against **null / permutation distributions** and **stability across resamples**: the
48	pattern must beat chance on something it was not fit to. Same principle, different
49	mechanism.
50	
51	## 3. Hypotheses
52	
53	Every hypothesis is **typed and falsifiable** — it names what is claimed, the test, the
54	data it runs on, and a pre-specified decision rule.
55	
56	- **Discovery mode:** the data surfaces candidate hypotheses; they are made specific,
57	  **locked, and timestamped before confirmation** (see Firewall B). The questions are
58	  data-derived, which is legitimate *because* discovery and confirmation use different
59	  data — not because the questions were guessed in advance.
60	- **Adjudicate-external-claims mode:** each external claim is itself a pre-stated,
61	  falsifiable assertion about the data. The adapter converts each into a typed hypothesis.
62	  These are tested against the blind baseline.
63	
64	"The claim" and "whether the claim is correct" are kept as separate objects throughout.
65	
66	## 4. Verdicts
67	
68	Confirmatory tests return one of **three** verdicts — never a forced binary:
69	
70	- **Supported** — the baseline corroborates the claim/hypothesis at the pre-specified
71	  threshold.
72	- **Contradicted** — the baseline is inconsistent with the claim at the pre-specified
73	  threshold.
74	- **Indeterminate** — the method cannot decide: underpowered, out of the measurement's
75	  reach, or the disagreement is plausibly a limitation of the method rather than of the
76	  claim. Indeterminate is a real outcome, structured like the others — not a failure.
77	
78	The third verdict is deliberate. A measure disagreeing with a claim is not automatically
79	proof the claim is wrong; it may be a limit of what the baseline can see. `indeterminate`
80	is the honest home for that case.
81	
82	## 5. Scoring the external source (adjudication mode)
83	
84	Once claims are adjudicated, the external source can be **scored against the validated

content.text:
Now I have enough to analyze. Let me think through the counter-arguments carefully.

**The core asymmetry:** In `confirm_whole_corpus`, the engine derives direction itself from the null distribution (`_empirical_p` for both predicted tail and opposite tail) — the caller supplies only `predicted_direction` (a tail label), and the engine *cross-checks* the statistic against the null. A claim predicting "less" with a statistic in the "greater" tail will register `p_contra <= alpha` → CONTRADICTED. The engine cannot be fooled.

In `verdict_from_pvalue` / `confirm_unit_level`, by contrast, the caller hands over a single `p_value` AND a pre-computed boolean `direction_supports_claim`. The engine does zero cross-checking. SUPPORTED-vs-CONTRADICTED is fully delegated.

Let me evaluate whether this delegation is *legitimate* for the unit-level case, which is the crux of the refutation attempt.

The counter-argument in the prompt: for a held-out unit-level test, the statistic's sign may not map to claim direction without a reference, so direction is legitimately the caller's to compute. Let me test this.

For a held-out unit-level test there's no null distribution passed in — the engine has only `statistic` and `p_value`. The `statistic` is opaque (could be a correlation, an accuracy delta, an effect size, a t-statistic). Whether a given numeric value of an opaque statistic "supports the claim" genuinely requires knowing what the claim predicted and what metric the statistic is. The engine truly *cannot* derive direction from `statistic` alone without imposing a model of what the statistic means. So the design decision to delegate is defensible in principle.

**But** the finding's strongest point is the repro's specific shape: `statistic` IS passed in and IS recorded in the verdict, yet it plays *no role* in the support/contradict decision and the deciding flag (`direction_supports_claim`) is *not* recorded in evidence. So the verdict is non-auditable: a reviewer sees `statistic=-99.0, label=SUPPORTED, p_value=0.001` with no recorded justification for why -99 supports the claim. The repro itself (statistic=-99 → SUPPORTED) is not in itself a bug if -99 legitimately supports a "predict-low" claim — it could be correct. The real defect is the *missing provenance*, not a miscomputed verdict.

Let me verify the evidence-recording gap precisely and check whether `direction_supports_claim` is anywhere recorded.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import verdict_from_pvalue
# whole-corpus records predicted_direction in evidence; unit-level does NOT record direction_supports_claim
v = verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=-99.0, p_value=0.001, alpha=0.05, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True)
print('evidence keys:', list(v.evidence.keys()))
print('direction flag recorded:', 'direction_supports_claim' in v.evidence)
"

content.content:
evidence keys: ['p_value']
direction flag recorded: False

content.text:
Confirmed. Both sub-claims of the finding hold:
1. The decision is made entirely from the caller-supplied boolean, with no engine cross-check against `statistic`.
2. `direction_supports_claim` is not recorded in evidence (only `p_value` is).

Now the severity question. Let me weigh the "caller is the trusted baseline toolkit" counter-argument and whether this is truly "incomplete fix of #2."

The issue #2 fix (per docstring) was about making direction matter so contradictions in the opposite tail are detectable. For **whole-corpus** that fix is real and structural (engine derives both tails). For **unit-level**, the "fix" amounts to threading a boolean the engine never validates — so the #2 principle ("a genuine contradiction is detectable by the engine") is *not* enforced on this path. The finding's framing of "incomplete fix of #2" is accurate for the unit-level path.

Is HIGH right? Considerations against HIGH:
- The caller is the trusted in-process baseline toolkit, not adversarial external input. A miscomputed direction is a caller bug, not an injection vector.
- Delegation of direction is *architecturally necessary* for unit-level (no reference available to the engine) — this is not a fixable-by-deriving-it situation, unlike whole-corpus.

Considerations for higher severity:
- This is a GRC/methodology integrity engine whose entire value proposition is non-falsifiable, auditable verdicts. A SUPPORTED verdict whose supporting-direction rationale is neither cross-checked nor recorded undermines the auditability guarantee — a verdict cannot be independently re-derived from its evidence. That is a methodology-integrity defect, the central concern of this codebase.

The genuinely fixable, defensible part is the **provenance gap** (record `direction_supports_claim` in evidence, like whole-corpus records `predicted_direction`). The "engine must cross-check direction from the statistic" part is largely *not* feasible for unit-level without imposing a metric model — so that portion of the finding's remedy is weak.

This makes it a PARTIAL: the existence of an unrecorded, un-cross-checked direction flag is a real defect (provenance/auditability), but the framing that the engine *should* derive direction from the statistic for the unit-level path is largely refuted by the architecture, and HIGH overstates it given the trusted-caller boundary.

VERDICT: PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: Overstated at HIGH; appropriate is MEDIUM (auditability/provenance defect, not an exploitable correctness hole). The deciding input is a trusted in-process toolkit boolean, not external/adversarial data, and — unlike whole-corpus — the engine genuinely cannot derive direction for a unit-level held-out test from an opaque `statistic` alone, so the "engine never cross-checks" half is partly inherent, not a clean bug.

REASONING:
- Repro reproduced exactly: `verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=-99.0, p_value=0.001, alpha=0.05, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True)` → `VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED`. (`/Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:63-99`.)
- Two factual sub-claims both hold. (1) The supported-vs-contradicted branch (lines 93-96) is decided solely by the `direction_supports_claim` boolean; `statistic` is recorded but never participates in the decision. (2) Evidence records only `{"p_value": ...}` (line 90); the deciding flag is not recorded — confirmed empirically (`evidence keys: ['p_value']`).
- The asymmetry is real and is the substance of "incomplete fix of #2": `confirm_whole_corpus` (lines 139-198) derives direction itself by computing empirical p-values in *both* the predicted and opposite tails against the supplied null (`_empirical_p`, lines 129-136; `p_contra`, line 168), so a statistic in the contradicting tail is independently caught (line 189 → CONTRADICTED) and `predicted_direction` is recorded in evidence (line 179). The unit-level path enforces none of that and records none of it. So the #2 principle ("a genuine contradiction is detectable" by the engine) is structurally enforced for whole-corpus but reduced to an unverified caller assertion for unit-level.
- Refutation that partly succeeds: for a held-out unit-level test the engine is given only `statistic` + `p_value` and no reference/null, and the statistic is an opaque metric (correlation, accuracy delta, t-stat, …). Mapping its numeric value to "supports/contradicts the claim" genuinely requires knowing the claim's predicted direction and the metric's semantics — which the engine does not have. So the finding's implied remedy ("engine should cross-check direction against the statistic") is largely infeasible here without imposing an unwanted metric model; the repro's `statistic=-99 → SUPPORTED` is not per se wrong (it is correct if the claim predicted a low value). The defect is therefore not a miscomputed verdict but an **un-auditable** one.
- Refutation that fails: the codebase's stated purpose is honest, independently re-derivable verdicts. A SUPPORTED verdict that records `statistic=-99.0, p_value=0.001` with no recorded direction rationale cannot be audited or replayed — a reviewer cannot tell why -99 supports the claim. Given whole-corpus already records `predicted_direction`, the omission on the unit-level path is an inconsistency and a genuine provenance gap, cheap to fix.
- Trusted-caller boundary lowers severity below HIGH: the wrong-direction outcome requires the trusted baseline toolkit to compute the boolean incorrectly (a caller bug), not hostile external input; there is no privilege/trust crossing here.

FIX_GUIDANCE: Do not attempt to have the engine infer direction from the bare `statistic` for the unit-level path — that is not feasible without a metric model and would impose unwanted assumptions; reject that part of the finding. Instead (1) record the deciding input in evidence so the verdict is auditable, mirroring whole-corpus: add `direction_supports_claim` (and ideally a caller-supplied short rationale/predicted-direction label) to the `evidence` dict in `verdict_from_pvalue` (line 90). (2) Optionally tighten the contract: require callers to pass the predicted-direction semantics alongside the statistic, or document explicitly in the docstring that direction is a trusted caller obligation and that `statistic` is recorded for provenance only (it does not gate the verdict) so reviewers are not misled into thinking it was cross-checked. Treat as MEDIUM.
